serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Fragrant Hotpot Chicken is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of braised and stir-fried chicken pieces with aromatics, served as a rice bowl or bento-style meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, stir-fried with vegetables and sauce — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked chicken and Chinese stir-fry preparations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Fragrant Hotpot Chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken (skin-on, stir-fried), cooking oil, soy sauce, and aromatics in proportions consistent with a typical Chinese fragrant hotpot chicken recipe. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce contribution. Values represent a plausible per-100 g profile for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed Chinese stir-fry dish with meat and sauce analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fragrant Hotpot Chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous Chinese mixed meat-and-sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains moderate carbohydrate from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar, but the dominant macronutrient is protein from chicken, which suppresses glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 27.3) / 100 ≈ 12.3, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 7.8 × 3.5 = 27.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 79% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~12 per serving) and added sugars from sauce components
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions; the low GI (estimated 45) and high protein content help moderate postprandial glucose response, but the sauce-derived sugars and carbohydrates should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate budgets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat content; cooking oil contribution to total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~1820 mg per serving) is the primary concern; saturated fat is moderate at 2.6 g per 100 g, and choosing skinless chicken and reduced-oil preparation would improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fragrant Hotpot Chicken (香锅鸡, Xiāng guō jī) is a Chinese-origin braised and stir-fried chicken dish characterised by its intensely aromatic, mildly spicy sauce made from dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is prepared by marinating chicken pieces and then wok-frying them at high heat with the spice blend until the sauce coats the meat and caramelises slightly, producing a rich, glossy finish. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a rice bowl or bento-style meal, as offered by restaurants such as First Bowl Rice Kitchen. The texture is tender and juicy with a slightly sticky, savoury-sweet sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (~16.5 g per 100 g) and provides moderate fat (~10.2 g per 100 g), with sodium being the primary dietary concern at approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to the soy and oyster sauce base. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without additional high-GI carbohydrate sides. Clinically, the dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension due to its high sodium load per serving.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Fragrant Hotpot Chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken and Chinese stir-fry sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar Chinese mixed meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the macronutrient profile is consistent with the dish category.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this composite estimate. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible USDA SR Legacy data was available. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg/100 g) reflecting the soy sauce and oyster sauce base typical of this dish. The dish is not confirmed halal as preparation practices at individual restaurants may vary. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for this specific dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore rice bowl portion sizes. Price range SGD 21.00–27.60 as recorded from GrabFood Singapore (First Bowl Rice Kitchen – Funan); this is a restaurant-level data point and not used as a nutritional source.
